title: Melting moments
subtitle: Cornflour shortbread sandwiched with jam and cream
excerpt: Melting moments are an Australian cornflour biscuit that earns its name: the ratio of cornflour to plain flour produces a crumb so short and fine it dissolves on the tongue. Sandwiched in pairs with raspberry jam and a little whipped cream or vanilla buttercream. No rolling, just piped or spooned rounds.
sourceNotes: Melting moments are a well-documented Australian and New Zealand biscuit, appearing in Women's Weekly and Edmonds cookbooks from the mid-twentieth century. The high proportion of cornflour gives them their characteristic texture: far more tender and crumbly than a standard shortbread. The sandwich filling varies: some versions use vanilla buttercream, others jam and cream. Both are traditional.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled biscuits in a single layer. Thaw and fill on the day of serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The unfilled biscuits keep well in an airtight tin for up to 5 days. Fill no more than 2 hours before serving as the cream softens the biscuits over time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; bake low and slow so they set without colouring, 13 to 15 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: The high proportion of cornflour (equal weight to plain flour in this recipe) makes a dough with almost no gluten structure. This is what produces the dissolving, melt-on-the-tongue texture. It also means the dough is very fragile when baked. Handle the hot biscuits with care; they firm up as they cool. Do not overbake: the biscuits should be pale cream all over, not golden, when they come out of the oven.

content.text: The key to melting moments is restraint: a low oven, a short bake, and handling the warm biscuits carefully. The
is very delicate when hot. Shape the dough with a fork dipped in flour to create the ridged pattern, or pipe with a star-tip nozzle for a more formal look.

Heat the oven to 160 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat 250 g softened butter with 60 g icing sugar and 1 tsp vanilla until very pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Sift in 150 g plain flour and 100 g cornflour and mix until a soft dough forms. Roll into walnut-sized balls, about 12 g each. Place on the trays with room to spread. Flatten gently with a fork dipped in flour, leaving ridges. Bake for 13 to 15 minutes until set and very pale cream; do not let them colour. Cool on the trays for 5 minutes, then transfer carefully to a rack to cool completely.

Match the cooled biscuits in pairs of similar size. Spread a little raspberry jam on one biscuit of each pair. Add a small amount of whipped cream on top of the jam. Press the second biscuit on top gently. Serve within 2 hours of filling.

items.solution: They were handled while too hot or the ratio is off. Wait until fully cool before sandwiching. The dough should use equal weights of plain flour and cornflour; too much cornflour makes them very fragile.

items.problem: Biscuits spread and lose their shape
items.solution: The butter was too soft, or the oven too hot. The butter should be soft enough to cream but not approaching melting. Chill the shaped biscuits on the trays for 10 minutes before baking if they are spreading.
